reasoning_model_1: The model generates the audio content 'This lecture is so boring, it makes me yawn' with high fidelity to the text script (Content Accuracy). The speech is fluent and natural, with no stuttering or electronic artifacts (Fluency). However, regarding the core requirement 'Non-Linguistic Vocalizations' (specifically a yawn), the audio is completely devoid of the requested feature. The sentence is read in a bright, energetic, and neutral tone throughout (0.0s - end), failing to convey any tiredness or the physical sound of a yawn as instructed. It ignores the paralinguistic command entirely.
reasoning_model_2: The generated audio begins with the spoken word 'Yawns' (or 'Yawns...') (approx 0.0s - 0.8s) before proceeding to the script 'this lecture is so boring...'. By vocalizing the stage direction/instruction 'Yawns' as a spoken word, the model fails Content Accuracy (addition of unrequested words) and breaks the Paralinguistic Compliance. Instead of performing the non-linguistic action (making a yawning sound), it reads the word describing the action. This is a hallucination of the prompt instructions into the speech content, resulting in an unnatural and incorrect generation.
model_comparison: Model 1 fails to implement the specific style (yawn) but successfully generates the correct text script with high fluency and naturalness. It effectively ignores the style constraint but provides usable speech. Model 2, in contrast, attempts the style but fails fundamentally by speaking the instruction ('Yawns') as text rather than performing the sound effect. This constitutes a content error and a failure to distinguish between linguistic content and non-linguistic vocalization. Model 1 is superior because a clean reading of the text is preferable to a hallucinated reading that inserts stage directions as spoken words.
